references.title: Early segmentation of students according to their academic performance: A predictive modelling approach

extracted.Courses: All courses in an European Faculty
extracted.Features: [Enrolment average grade, Enrolment exams average grade, Average grade in the first semester, Average grade in the second semester, Number of ECTS completed in the second semester,  Number of ECTS completed in the first semester, Average # of exams attended to conclude the courses in the first academic year, High school average grade, Degree, Mother's education level, Father's education level, Mother's job, Father's job, Academic year of enrolment, Enrolment option, Marital status, School type, Gender, Enrolment stage]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Data refers to multiple years and is provided by the university as is.
extracted.Models: Naive Bayes, SVM, DT, Random Forest, Bagging, AdaBoost
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Accuracy: 0.961 - RF, Macro Recall: 0,9566 - AdaBoost, Macro Precision: 0,9728 - RF}
extracted.Comments:
Authors do multiclass classification to predict end of program cluster labels.

The work is followed by a set of feature interpretability sections.

One cool finding is that the performance in the first year seems to be a pretty good indicator of performance at the end of the program → good students tend to continue being good… worse students continue being worse.
extracted.Limitations:
Sampling bias: one faculty with a specific set of students.

Does not consider e.g. LMS interactions.

annotations.text: Authors use a 2-stage system with data collected from the first year of a program to predict whether the students will be successful or not. In this context, success is defined in 2 terms:

More than developing a predictive model and testing it on data collected throughout 12 years, the authors also propose a segmentation of students. Results show that the RF model is effective at predicting success AND that the segmentation may be useful in the development of optimal strategies to promote high achievement and mitigate student failure.
